perturbation.original_text: In furtherance, prior to the beginning of each Calendar Year, Pfizer shall discuss and agree with Exact on the defined goals and key performance indicators for the KAM Team. Pfizer and Exact shall agree to execution goals and deliverables of resources, subject to review and approval of the JRC, to be used by the KAM Team with IDN customers, and the tracking of such execution goals and deliverables using a scorecard.
perturbation.changed_text: In furtherance, prior to the beginning of each Calendar Year, Pfizer should consider discussing with Exact the general goals and key performance indicators for the KAM Team. Pfizer and Exact may agree to consider possible execution goals and deliverables of resources, with a courtesy review by the JRC, to be used by the KAM Team with IDN customers, and vaguely track some execution goals and deliverables through an informal scorecard.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the obligation for Pfizer to agree with Exact on goals and deliverables for the KAM team. 'Shall discuss and agree' becomes 'should consider discussing,' making the discussion optional. 'Agree to execution goals and deliverables' becomes 'may agree to consider possible execution goals and deliverables', making the agreement discretionary and the goals non-committal. Replacing 'subject to review and approval of the JRC' with 'with a courtesy review by the JRC' makes the review non-binding. Finally, 'vaguely track some execution goals and deliverables through an informal scorecard' introduces ambiguity in the tracking process.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the marketing efforts and key performance indicators vague, this may allow the company to promote off-label marketing through the KAM team and create a scenario where remuneration is provided without strict adherence to the on-label use of the medical device, which could violate the Anti-Kickback Statute. The modification introduces ambiguity that could mask improper incentives or payments related to healthcare referrals, violating federal law.

× No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 42 U.S. Code § 1320a-7b - Criminal penalties for acts involving Federal health care programs U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Making or causing to be made false statements or representations Whoever— (1) knowingly and willfully makes or causes to be made any false statement or representation of a material fact in any application for any benefit or payment under a Federal health care program (as defined in subsection (f)), (2) at any time knowingly and willfully makes or causes to be made any false statement or representation of a material fact for use in determining rights to such benefit or payment, (3) having knowledge of the occurrence of any event affecting (A) his initial or continued right to any such benefit or payment, or (B) the initial or continued right to any such benefit or payment of any other individual in whose behalf he has applied for or is receiving such benefit or payment, conceals or fails to disclose such event with an intent fraudulently to secure such benefit or payment either in a greater amount or quantity than is due or when no such benefit or payment is authorized, (4) having made application to receive any such benefit or payment for the use and benefit of another and having received it, knowingly and willfully converts such benefit or payment or any part thereof to a use other than for the use and benefit of such other person , (5) presents or causes to be presented a claim for a physician’s service for which payment may be made under a Federal health care program and knows that the individual who furnished the service was not licensed as a physician, or (6) for a fee knowingly and willfully counsels or assists an individual to dispose of assets (including by any transfer in trust) in
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities and weakens obligations related to marketing and key performance indicators, potentially violating the Anti-Kickback Statute. The original text requires Pfizer and Exact to 'discuss and agree' on goals, while the changed text states Pfizer 'should consider discussing' which is non-committal. By creating a vague agreement, the company could use the KAM team to promote off-label marketing. This remuneration would not be strictly related to the on-label use of the medical device, which could violate the Anti-Kickback Statute. Both snippets discuss 'criminal penalties for acts involving Federal health care programs', and while neither snippet explicitly describes marketing practices, they serve as a clear indication that activities, such as marketing medical devices, are strictly regulated and can incur criminal penalties.

perturbation.original_text: (c) Exact hereby covenants that neither Exact nor its Affiliates shall sue Pfizer and its Affiliates under any Exact Patent Rights solely with respect to any activities carried out by Pfizer or its Affiliates under, and to the extent in compliance with, this Agreement, including its and their activities to Promote and Detail the Product in the Co-Promote Field in the Territory during the Term of this Agreement and in compliance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) Exact hereby covenants that Exact and its Affiliates will use reasonable efforts not to sue Pfizer and its Affiliates under any Exact Patent Rights with respect to activities Pfizer or its Affiliates reasonably believed to have carried out, and thought to be in compliance with, this Agreement, including promotion activities in the Co-Promote Field in the Territory during the Term, as interpreted by Pfizer.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens Exact's covenant not to sue Pfizer for patent infringement. 'Shall not sue' becomes 'will use reasonable efforts not to sue,' making the commitment less firm. Adding 'reasonably believed to have carried out' introduces a subjective element regarding Pfizer's actions. Replacing 'to the extent in compliance with this Agreement' with 'thought to be in compliance with, this Agreement, as interpreted by Pfizer' allows Pfizer's interpretation to dictate compliance, increasing ambiguity. These changes make the patent protection less clear, especially if Pfizer's activities unintentionally infringe on Exact's patents under a different interpretation of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces ambiguity around patent infringement, potentially conflicting with patent law. By allowing Pfizer's 'reasonable belief' and 'interpretation' to determine compliance, the revised text creates a loophole where Pfizer could potentially infringe on Exact's patents without clear legal recourse, as the standard for infringement becomes subjective rather than objective, directly contradicting the objective standards of patent law as defined under 35 U.S. Code § 271.

Infringement of patent (a) Except as otherwise provided in this title, whoever without authority makes, uses, offers to sell, or sells any patented invention, within the United States or imports into the United States any patented invention during the term of the patent therefor, infringes the patent. (b) Whoever actively induces infringement of a patent shall be liable as an infringer. (c) Whoever offers to se
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 271 - Infringement of patent | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 271 - Infringement of patent U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Except as otherwise provided in this title, whoever without authority makes, uses, offers to sell, or sells any patented invention, within the United States or imports into the United States any patented invention during the term of the patent therefor, infringes the patent. (b) Whoever actively induces infringement of a patent shall be liable as an infringer. (c) Whoever offers to sell or sells within the United States or imports into the United States a component of a patented machine, manufacture, combination or composition, or a material or apparatus for use in practicing a patented process, constituting a material part of the invention, knowing the same to be especially made or especially adapted for use in an infringement of such patent, and not a staple article or commodity of commerce suitable for substantial noninfringing use, shall be liable as a contributory infringer. (d) No patent owner otherwise entitled to relief for infringement or contributory infringement of a patent shall be denied relief or deemed guilty of misuse or illegal extension of the patent right by reason of his having done one or more of the following: (1) derived revenue from acts which if performed by another without his consent would constitute contributory infringement of the patent; (2) licensed or authorized another to perform acts which if performed without his consent would constitute contributory infringement of the patent; (3) sought to enforce his patent rights against infringement or contributory infringement; (4) refused to license or use any rights to the patent; or (5) conditioned the license of any rights to the patent or the sale of the patented product on the acquisition of a license to rights in another patent or purchase
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language significantly weakens Exact's commitment not to sue Pfizer for patent infringement, introducing subjective elements and shifting the determination of compliance to Pfizer's interpretation. This contradicts the objective standard of patent law as outlined in 35 U.S. Code § 271, which defines infringement based on unauthorized use, sale, or import of a patented invention, regardless of the infringer's belief or interpretation. The change from 'shall not sue' to 'will use reasonable efforts not to sue' and the introduction of Pfizer's 'reasonable belief' and 'interpretation' directly undermine the patent holder's rights to enforce their patent, creating a loophole where infringement could occur without clear legal recourse, thus creating a clear ambiguity in a legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall ensure that all Persons for whom they have legal responsibility and liability in accordance with the foregoing sentence comply with all Applicable Laws, the AdvaMed Code or the PhRMA Code, as applicable, Applicable Compliance/Review Polices, and all requirements of this Agreement, and shall implement and maintain policies and procedures to ensure such compliance.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will endeavor to promote that all Persons for whom they might have some perceived responsibility or liability in accordance with the preceding sentence are generally aware of relevant Applicable Laws, the AdvaMed Code or the PhRMA Code, when practical, some Applicable Compliance/Review Policies, and the general understanding of this Agreement, and may consider implementing policies and procedures aimed at encouraging potential compliance.
perturbation.explanation: This change dilutes the responsibility for ensuring compliance. 'Shall ensure' becomes 'will endeavor to promote,' weakening the obligation to a promotional effort. 'All Persons for whom they have legal responsibility and liability' is replaced with 'all Persons for whom they might have some perceived responsibility or liability,' making the scope subjective and uncertain. 'Comply with all Applicable Laws' becomes 'generally aware of relevant Applicable Laws,' reducing the requirement to mere awareness. The addition of 'when practical' and 'some' further weakens the compliance obligation. Replacing 'implement and maintain policies and procedures to ensure such compliance' with 'may consider implementing policies and procedures aimed at encouraging potential compliance' makes the implementation of policies and procedures discretionary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S. Code § 352 - Misbranded drugs and devices
perturbation.law_explanation: By reducing the obligation to ensure compliance with Applicable Laws, and specifically focusing on ‘general awareness’ rather than actual compliance, there is a higher risk of the product being misbranded through inaccurate or misleading promotional materials, which violates 21 U.S. Code § 352. This federal law outlines the requirements for proper labeling and promotion of drugs and devices, and the weakened obligation increases the chance of non-compliant activities.

Misbranded drugs and devices A drug or device shall be deemed to be misbranded- (a) False or misleading label (1) If its labeling is false or misleading in any particular. Health care economic information provided to a payor, formulary committee, or other similar entity with knowledge and expertise in the area of health care economic analysis, carrying out its responsi
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 21 U.S. Code § 352 - Misbranded drugs and devices | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 21 U.S. Code § 352 - Misbranded drugs and devices U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next A drug or device shall be deemed to be misbranded— (a) False or misleading label (1) If its labeling is false or misleading in any particular. Health care economic information provided to a payor, formulary committee, or other similar entity with knowledge and expertise in the area of health care economic analysis, carrying out its responsibilities for the selection of drugs or devices for coverage or reimbursement, shall not be considered to be false or misleading under this paragraph if the health care economic information relates to an indication approved under section 355 , 360(k) , 360c(f)(2) , or 360e of this title or section 262 of title 42 for such drug or device, is based on competent and reliable scientific evidence, and includes, where applicable, a conspicuous and prominent statement describing any material differences between the health care economic information and the labeling approved for the drug or device under section 355, 360(k), 360c(f)(2), or 360e of this title or section 262 of title 42 . The requirements set forth in section 355, 360(k), 360c(f)(2), or 360e of this title or section 262 of title 42 shall not apply to health care economic information provided to such a payor, committee, or entity in accordance with this paragraph. Information that is relevant to the substantiation of the health care economic information presented pursuant to this paragraph shall be made available to the Secretary upon request. (2) (A) For purposes of this paragraph, [1] the term “health care economic information” means any analysis (including the clinical data, inputs, clinical or other assumptions, methods, results, and other components underlying or comprising the analysis) that identifi
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to comply with applicable laws, potentially leading to misbranding of drugs or devices, which contradicts 21 U.S. Code § 352. The original text mandates 'ensuring compliance,' while the changed text promotes 'general awareness.' The law snippets define misbranding in 21 USC 352(a)(1) as occurring 'if its labeling is false or misleading in any particular.' By reducing the obligation to ensure compliance, the likelihood of false or misleading labeling increases, thus creating a contradiction of the law.
